video_title: The Market Is Splitting In Two — How Do We Navigate it?
gist: Avi Felman and Jonah Vanberg argue the market is K-shaped/splitting in two — a narrow AI-and-memory-driven equity melt-up alongside a deteriorating crypto-majors complex — and advocate high cash plus concentration in single names, narrative-driven sector rotation (software shorts, healthcare/biotech longs, robotics, energy), and a battery of crypto pair trades (XMR/ZEC, IBIT/MSTR) to capture record dispersion while managing late-cycle risk.

insights.claim: The market is splitting into a K-shape: winners are being held onto while losers get puked out via tax-loss harvesting, producing a two-tier market where a handful of AI/memory names melt up and the majors lag.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: Index exposure understates the divergence; investors should select the narrow set of winners rather than own the benchmark.
insights.quote: I think we're in a K-shaped uh the mic market is splitting in two basically.

insights.claim: The S&P is up ~7% YTD but the Mag 7 leaders that drove the prior year's gains are now sputtering — Nvidia only up ~10%, Microsoft and Meta struggling — signaling the crazy late stage of the bull where derivative plays run instead of the direct leaders.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Leadership narrowing/fatigue is a classic late-cycle tell; raise cash and wait for better entries rather than chase.
insights.quote: the larger assets are struggling to rally right now. that that makes me worried

insights.claim: When retail piles into derivative/catch-up trades (HP, Dell) rather than the direct AI play, it is a signal the rally is nearing exhaustion.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Watch breadth of leadership: derivatives outperforming the primary names is a sell-discipline trigger.
insights.quote: one way to tell that the rally is coming to an end is when people are buying the things that are derivative plays and not necessarily the direct play itself

insights.claim: Today's equity rally is leverage-driven with extremely high dispersion and weak breadth, so holders are mostly weak hands chasing momentum — making the market far more vulnerable to shocks than the healthier, strong-hand market of April.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Weak-hand ownership raises crash risk on any catalyst (Iran, inflation); size and hedge accordingly.
insights.quote: A lot of the people that are holding assets now are in it because they are going up

insights.claim: RKLB roughly doubling its market cap from $4.5B to $9B on the back of a single $90M contract exemplifies retail money chasing and excess liquidity sloshing around late in the cycle.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Wildly disproportionate cap moves on small fundamentals flag froth; Avi exited RKLB after the run.
insights.quote: It literally went from a 4.5 to9 billion market cap because it signed a $90 million contract. And I mean, that's just nuts.

insights.claim: The current alt-season-style melt-up is analogous to EOS running in April 2018 after Bitcoin had already topped — i.e. excess liquidity rotating into derivatives is a late-cycle topping signature.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Historical analog warns that the broadening rally may be the last gasp before a top.
insights.quote: it's like the equivalent of EOS running in April of 2018 after Bitcoin had topped

themes.theme: Positioning: cash + concentration in single names

insights.claim: The optimal late-cycle posture is high cash plus high concentration in a few single names you believe in, so you stay allocated to the bull market while holding dry powder to buy dips.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Barbell of concentrated conviction longs and cash is the prescribed playbook for a volatile, splitting market.
insights.quote: the best way to navigate these markets when they're high volume is to have those single names that you think are going to do really well and then have cash so that you can buy the dips

insights.claim: Buying the index is a mistake because the bull market lives in only ~30 companies; owning the rest of the NASDAQ 100 / S&P 500 dilutes exposure to where the economy is actually growing.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Argues against passive indexing here; concentrate in the growth cohort and hold cash instead of the laggards.
insights.quote: the reality is the bull market is in like 30 companies. And so why would you allocate to the rest of it

insights.claim: Traders sitting on large unrealized gains (UPNL) in memory/compute/AI-adjacent stocks should take profits — 'if you traded like a god, it's time to pay yourself like a god' — rather than ride the position indefinitely.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Profit-taking discipline after 3x-10x runs; don't let unrealized gains become a round trip.
insights.quote: it's time to pay yourself like a god. You can't just you can't just sit on that UPNL forever.

insights.claim: Avi's go-forward style is shifting to highly tactical trading rather than buy-and-hold, accepting the 'time in market beats timing the market' counterargument but rejecting it because volatility favors active traders.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Signals a regime where active rotation is expected to outperform passive holding.
insights.quote: my my general approach now is is going to be very tactical relative to the to the past

themes.theme: Memory/DRAM as the leading indicator of the AI chip cycle

insights.claim: Physical DDR4 memory pricing is the most leading, 'squeeziest' indicator of the AI/compute boom — DDR4 has the biggest physical shortage and will lead every other AI-related asset.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: Track a DDR4 index as the upstream tell; AI-equity tops should follow memory rolling over, not the other way around.
insights.quote: To me this is the most leading indicator. This is physical demand. This is like as leading as it gets.

insights.claim: Per DRAM Exchange data, a DDR4 16Gb module went from ~$8.50 last August to a ~$77-78 peak (roughly a 9x), sold off ~30% (from ~$80 to ~$58) between March and May, then resumed rising ~10% in the last week.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: The reacceleration after a 30% pullback supports staying long memory-levered names; a sustained downtrend would be the sell signal.
insights.quote: the price of a DDR416 gigabyte gigabit module was let's call it $8.50 then it topped out around $778 bucks

insights.claim: Dell is not in a bubble at 23x forward PE (vs ~50x trailing) because earnings are rising on new AI-related sales; forward earnings only revert if the DDR4 chart fully rolls over.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: Forward, not trailing, PE is the right lens; Dell stays a buy unless memory prices collapse.
insights.quote: I'm not worried about Dell trading at 23 times forward earnings unless those forward earnings are going to revert to what they looked like in the past

insights.claim: Dell stock traced the inverse of DDR4: it trended down through Oct-Dec while DDR4 went vertical, bottomed around $110-125 in February, then ran straight to ~$475 a share.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: Illustrates the lead-lag: the commodity led, the equity followed with a delay — a template for finding mispriced AI plays.
insights.quote: It bottoms out in February at $125, $110 a share, then that's it. Then it goes straight up to $475 a share.

insights.claim: Most fundamentals are mean-reverting (higher prices bring supply, dampen demand) unless a paradigm shift breaks the range, so the key analytical task is identifying the underlying fundamental asset and judging whether it has broken its range.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: Distinguishes durable secular moves from reversion traps; apply the 'has the range broken?' test before chasing.
insights.quote: most fundamentals are mean reverting right like econ 101

themes.theme: Bitcoin breakdown and the Saylor/MSTR unwind

insights.claim: Bitcoin's technical setup is terrible: it broke down out of a 62-71k range it held Feb-April, and the bullish breakout reversed once the Saylor-driven flywheel failed.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Failed range breakout plus broken reflexive bid argues for further downside in BTC.
insights.quote: the the technical analysis here looks really terrible as well

insights.claim: The MSTR/STRC front-running trade — buying ahead of Saylor's predictable Bitcoin purchases — worked for one month, then in month two markets adjusted and traders used Saylor's buying as exit liquidity, and he bought far less than before as people recognized the product as Ponzi-like.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: The collapse of the reflexive corporate bid removes a key pillar under BTC's prior rally.
insights.quote: people use a trade as exit liquidity they use sailor's buying to basically get out

insights.claim: Saylor/MicroStrategy is facing a survival crisis, signaled by selling Bitcoin in escalating tranches ('$35 worth... $2 billion worth'); the unwind is only in its first inning, so Avi will not re-buy Bitcoin until Saylor is finished blowing up.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: An MSTR forced-deleveraging cascade could be a continued source of BTC supply; wait for it to exhaust before buying.
insights.quote: He's obviously facing a survival crisis... I'm not going to buy Bitcoin in the reby Bitcoin in the first inning of that unwind.

insights.claim: MicroStrategy trades at only 1.2x NAV and that premium likely flips to a discount soon as holders get blown up.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: A premium-to-discount compression is the engine of the long-IBIT/short-MSTR trade.
insights.quote: Micro Strategy is still trading at 1.2x NAV. That doesn't seem like it's going to last. I bet it goes to a discount pretty soon

insights.claim: Avi sold the Bitcoin lots he had bought above $70k to harvest a tax loss (crypto has no wash-sale rule, BTC is property under IRS) but deliberately did not re-buy six seconds later, choosing to wait for the Saylor unwind to finish.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Tax-loss harvesting in crypto is frictionless (no wash rule), and the choice not to rebuy is itself a bearish BTC signal.
insights.quote: I didn't buy it back 6 seconds later.

insights.claim: Year-end seasonality: crypto tends to fall harder in the last week of December as holders dump assets for tax purposes and rebuy in the new year, creating a recurring tradable dip.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: A calendar edge: fade December tax-loss selling and position for the January rebuy.
insights.quote: the end of the year, like last week of December, things tend to go down a lot more because people sell out all of their assets and then reby them back in the new year

insights.claim: Crypto is showing the most dispersion ever — hype, Zcash and VVV crushing it while Bitcoin, Ethereum and the majors do very poorly — driven partly by quantum fears pushing flows into 'quantum-resistant' names.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Record dispersion rewards relative-value/pair trading over directional beta.
insights.quote: a market that maybe has the most dispersion that I've ever seen in my entire life

insights.claim: Large crypto allocators remain overweight majors (SOL, ETH, BTC) purely because of liquidity constraints ($100M can't buy size in alts), but a convergence is underway as good altcoins grow market cap relative to the majors.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Structural overweight in majors plus alt convergence supports long-alt / short-major pair positioning.
insights.quote: the market right now is still very overweight majors... all the large allocators are still in soul, they're in ETH, they're they're in BTC. And the reason is because of liquidity.

insights.claim: TON is outperforming on a real catalyst — Pavel Durov renaming it (to 'Gram') and deepening Telegram integration with a path to monetization — making it a fundamentally-backed alt rather than pure narrative.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Distinguishes catalyst-driven alts (TON) from thinner narrative plays (NEAR) when picking longs.
insights.quote: ton's outperformed over the last little bit because Pavle is renaming it Graham he's going to be integrating it more into telegram

insights.claim: Recommended hedge structure: express bearish-BTC views as pair trades (short Bitcoin / long outperforming alts) rather than outright shorts to capture upside while protecting downside amid high dispersion.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Pair trades are the prescribed vehicle for this dispersion regime; reduces directional market risk.
insights.quote: if you're nervous about Bitcoin going down, just put them on as pair trades. Go short Bitcoin, go long these assets.

insights.claim: Specific pair trade: long XMR / short ZEC — Monero is up 10% vs Zcash today and likely has another ~50% to go, since XMR offers privacy and will be quantum-resistant far before BTC.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Concrete relative-value idea with a target (~50% more XMR/ZEC upside) on the privacy/quantum theme.
insights.quote: I think that XMR is probably it's up 10% against ZEC today. I think it probably has like another 50% to go against ZEC.

insights.claim: Specific pair trade: long IBIT / short MSTR (notional-neutral) — easiest to execute on Robinhood since collateralizing with on-chain BTC is harder; bets the MSTR NAV premium converts to a discount.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: A flagged, trackable trade to capture MSTR premium compression vs spot BTC exposure.
insights.quote: long IBIT short MSTR... on Robin Hood. That that's probably the trade

themes.theme: Narrative rotation into equities: software, healthcare, robotics, energy

insights.claim: Crypto traders should apply their narrative/flow frameworks to equities: in a retail-driven bull, buy under-allocated or heavily-shorted sectors — which is exactly why software ripped 25% as shorts got blown out.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: A transferable edge: hunt heavily-shorted sectors for squeeze longs in a flow-driven market.
insights.quote: look for the heavily shorted sectors and buy them because if retail is driving the market and flows are driving the market

insights.claim: After software's 25% short-squeeze, shorting software is now an attractive entry because software businesses will be heavily impaired by AI over the next three years — a mega-trend.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Use the squeeze as a higher-level entry to short a structurally AI-disrupted sector.
insights.quote: shorting software here, that's a mega trend... over the next 3 years, software businesses are going to be heavily impacted by AI

insights.claim: Worldcoin, up 35% today, is likely a short entry on the same logic of fading parabolic narrative pops.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: A specific short candidate flagged off an extreme one-day move.
insights.quote: World Coin is probably an entry right now because it's up 35% today. Maybe you want to start building a short there.

insights.claim: Healthcare/biotech is the most overlooked, underperforming sector that benefits massively from AI (drug development), making it Avi's next research focus — e.g. Novo Nordisk down ~44% in 2025 to a 4-year-low PE of ~14.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Rotation candidate: beaten-down pharma with AI tailwinds and cheap multiples.
insights.quote: healthcare actually benefits massively from AI. Drug development benefits massively from AI.

insights.claim: Health insurers are an underappreciated AI winner: as new drugs (e.g. a just-announced cholesterol cure) cut long-term care costs, the cost of servicing long-dated contracts falls — e.g. Oscar Health is down 50% from 2025 highs despite guiding to large 2026 growth.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Second-order AI play: long beaten-down managed-care insurers on falling future claims costs.
insights.quote: insurance companies I think are actually going to be huge winners of the AI revolution specifically because as health care outcomes improve cost comes down

insights.claim: Robotics is set to be a 'massive mega bubble' with almost no listed access today except Robbo Strategy (a stock, not a token); Avi is scaling into robotics and space on the next drawdown.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Early positioning in a thinly-accessible bubble theme; accumulate on dips.
insights.quote: I do think robotics is going to be a pretty massive mega bubble and there kind of no ways to get access to it right now except for Robbo strategy

insights.claim: On the next market scare Avi plans to scale into space and robotics, buy Mag 7 (calling them screaming buys if they fall another 20%), buy less memory and more energy, and buy less Intel than he did during the Iran war.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: A concrete dip-buying shopping list and rotation (out of memory, into energy/robotics/space).
insights.quote: I'm scaling into robotics and I'm probably buying less memory and more energy

insights.claim: Uranium is a long-term energy thesis; after distribution, the level to 'load the boat' again is ~42.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: A defined buy level for uranium on the energy/AI-power demand theme.
insights.quote: if we if we get down to a 42 level, that's, you know, load load the boat there again

themes.theme: Macro crosscurrents: AI supply wall, Iran/oil, the Fed

insights.claim: Up to ~$3 trillion of new AI 'supply' is about to hit the market when OpenAI and Anthropic come public, which will definitionally dampen the rally because part of today's bid is just people wanting AI exposure they currently can't access directly.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: A coming supply event is a structural headwind/top risk; expect a slowdown around those IPOs, possibly after one final push.
insights.quote: we're about to have once open AAI and Anthropic come on, maybe up to $3 trillion of additional supply for the AI thesis that comes on the market

insights.claim: The 'market tops on a massive AI supply event' view is consensus among smart allocators but NOT priced or understood by the retail majority who just want AI exposure — so the supply effect can still bite.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: The non-consensus retail blind spot means the supply shock retains downside power despite being 'known'.
insights.quote: while that may be true among smart traders and allocators, I don't think that's true among the vast majority of people that are trading in this market

insights.claim: Oil is rising again on Iran escalation (Iranian drones damaged Kuwait's airport, fresh US strikes on Qeshm Island, talks breaking down), a threat vector markets have largely forgotten and which is more dangerous now than in April given the weaker, weak-hand market.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Re-escalation risk is underpriced; an oil spike into a fragile market is the key tail risk to hedge.
insights.quote: the world of markets has forgotten about Iran but that's another that's another threat vector

insights.claim: Jonah's biggest position is the bet that the world will not 'stock out' of oil into a depression; he concedes his timing on the war's duration has been terrible but the expression of the trade has been fantastic as equities melted up.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: Conviction that no global oil stock-out occurs underwrites his equity-bullish stance; the risk is a $200 crude scenario.
insights.quote: we are not going to stock out of oil globally and allow this to persist into a you know dire economic depression scenario

insights.claim: An actual global oil stock-out sending crude to ~$200 would crash equities 30-40% — 'sayonara mag 7, sayonara Dell, sayonara memory' — because oil sits far below AI tokens on the hierarchy of needs.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: Frames the asymmetric downside: the AI trade is conditional on cheap energy; an oil shock dominates everything.
insights.quote: you can bet your boots that the stock market will trade down 30 40% if there's like an actual global oil stock out and the price of a barrel of crude goes to 200 bucks

insights.claim: Trump publicly threatening Fed-pick Kevin Warsh ('you better do a good job'), plus a reportedly tense Trump-Netanyahu call, add political/policy uncertainty that compounds inflation and consumption data as rally headwinds.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Fed-independence and geopolitical noise are additional reasons to expect a near-term slowdown.
insights.quote: Trump already attacking Kevin Worsh... He's like, "You better you better do a good job."

themes.theme: Trading frameworks: holder analysis, lead-lag, and AI-as-tool

insights.claim: The single most important daily exercise is determining who holds each asset and why: ideal longs are those held by deep-value investors who won't sell even on a 5x but that will eventually hit a narrative — exactly how memory played out.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: A reusable selection rule: prefer assets with strong-hand ownership and a latent narrative catalyst.
insights.quote: the optimal way to to to to trade is to hold is to hold an asset that you think is going to hit a narrative at some point in the future but right now is being held by deep value investors

insights.claim: Actionable AI workflow: find the physical/fundamental leading indicator (e.g. a DDR4 index) for the sector you care about, map its lead-lag to the target basket, and sanity-check with your own brain to avoid overfitting.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: A repeatable research process to surface mispriced sector plays using commodity lead indicators.
insights.quote: I would try to find the commodity that represents the leading indicator for the sector or the asset or the basket of assets that you care about

insights.claim: AI is far more useful when connected to proprietary/live data than when reasoning from training data alone — the claim is you source ~10x as many opportunities per hour with data-connected AI.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Edge increasingly comes from data-connected AI tooling for idea sourcing and backtesting.
insights.quote: if your AI is connected to proprietary data sources, you source 10 times as many opportunities per hour

insights.claim: Beware 'step 1: AI, step 2: ?, step 3: profit' — an unstructured prompt about a euphoric thesis will get the AI to self-reinforce your bias; you must guide it and verify the data yourself.
insights.speaker: Jonah Vanberg
insights.so_what: Risk-management caution on AI-driven research: structure prompts and human-check outputs to avoid confirmation bias.
insights.quote: an unstructured prompt about, you know, a euphoric future in pharma stocks, the AI will just self-reinforce your ideas and tell you to buy the ball

insights.claim: Crypto remains the most retail-democratized, inefficient market — retail can touch instruments TradFi desks cannot — so the edge advice is to 'delete the word crypto', call yourself a trader, and extend the same skills to equities.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Identity/skill reframing: crypto-trained narrative and flow skills transfer to equities where retail now has an edge.
insights.quote: delete the word crypto and just just branch out in other things where you can where you can be a retail trader

insights.claim: Crypto-protocol employees affiliated with fading L1s (Hedera/HBAR, Polkadot, Cardano) should quit and pivot to AI or to crypto products users actually want, because the asset class has bifurcated against the old majors.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Career/capital signal: legacy L1 protocols are viewed as structurally losing; rotate human and financial capital out.
insights.quote: If you work in crypto, especially if you work for a protocol like, let's just say, HAR, Polka Dot, Cardano... quit your job.
